text: [warm] Evening to you. [gently] Don't mind that you can't see me — I'm just the memory of a man who loved this walk. Did it most every evening, right up till the end. Let's take it together, one more time. [unhurried] No need to hurry. The dead have all the time in the world — and tonight, so do you. [walking, fond] Turn your face toward the park and come along. Easy now. Mind the roots — these live oaks have been buckling this sidewalk since before your grandparents drew breath. The city could grind 'em flat and pour it smooth. She never does. Some things you walk around, not over. [pause] Stop here a moment. [softly] Look up. See how the moss hangs so still? They call it Spanish moss, though it's neither Spanish nor moss — just an old gray beard the oaks've been growing three hundred years. When the wind moves it, the old folks said that was the trees remembering. [warm] I always liked that. [walking] Go on, walk on slow. Look up under these porch ceilings as you pass — see that pale blue? Haint blue, we call it. Folks painted it to fool the haints — the restless spirits — make 'em think the ceiling was sky, or water, so they couldn't cross and trouble the house. [chuckles softly] A whole city, painting its porches against the dead. [gently] Never kept me out, though. But then — I'm no haint. Just a memory. And memory goes where it pleases. [pointing] That lamp on the corner — that's a real flame, not a bulb. Savannah's kept a few of 'em burning since the eighteen-hundreds. Watch how it throws your shadow out long behind you. On a night like this I used to think every one of those shadows was somebody who'd walked here before. [pause] Maybe they were. [fond] Smell that? Tea olive, and somebody's jasmine gone wild over a fence. And underneath it, if the wind's right — the river. Mud and salt and time. That's the smell of Savannah remembering herself. You won't ever forget it now. [approaching, slowing] Up ahead, where the street opens and the dark turns green and wide — that's Forsyth. The big park. I'll only walk you to the edge of it. That's as far as I go. [softly] My walk always ended right here. I'd stand at the tree line, say my evening prayer, and turn back for home. Yours is just beginning. [reassuring] When you step in under those trees, you won't be alone. Your guide'll be right there — and so will others. Memories, same as me. Don't fear 'em. They only want what we all want: to be remembered. [warm] Nothing out here can harm you. Nothing follows you home. [blessing] Go on now. The park's waiting. [softly] And so is she.
